The 5 Cs of Netflix
Week 11, Day 1 — MKTG 2080
Professor Only
Quick Navigation
Exercise Overview Student Screens Answer Key Concept Map Discussion Q&As Common Mistakes Extensions Timeline Debrief Answers
Section 1
Exercise Overview

Students apply the 5 Cs pricing framework to Netflix's tiered subscription strategy. Each student works on their own device and goes through three phases per C: answering individually, stopping to discuss with their group, and then recording the group's consensus. This structured flow ensures all voices are heard and reveals how company objectives, customer willingness to pay, cost structure, competitive positioning, and channel relationships shape Netflix's pricing decisions.

Key Parameters

Exercise Type
Guided 5C Analysis
Duration
~25–30 minutes
Setup
Individual device, groups of 2–5
Email Required
UC email (@uc.edu)
Password
FIVE5C
Individual Min
20 chars
Consensus Min
30 chars
Teaching Angle The tension in this exercise is that each C has a defensible "right" answer for Netflix, but the Cs interact — students who analyze each C in isolation miss how Company Objectives shapes everything else. The debrief should surface these connections. For example, Netflix's Company Objectives (dual growth: subscribers + revenue per user) explain why they can justify the Customers pricing gap, which their Costs structure (fixed content) enables, even against Competition, because Channel Members (advertisers) subsidize the low tier.
Design Intent: Discussion Gates The STOP discussion phases between individual and consensus answers deliberately break the "first person talks, everyone agrees" pattern. When students share their individual answers and hear different perspectives before writing the group answer, they synthesize multiple viewpoints instead of anchoring on one. This results in richer consensus answers and surfaces disagreement — the best debrief hooks come from groups that had to negotiate different interpretations of the same C.
Section 1.5
What Students See: Screen-by-Screen Walkthrough
Quick Reference These screenshots show every screen in order, with sample student data filled in. Use this to preview the exercise without entering all the answers yourself.
Password Gate

Students see this first. Password is FIVE5C (given in class).

Join Screen

Name, UC email (validated), and group number. All required before starting.

Netflix Context (top)

NEW: Case-light Netflix background so students aren't confused by the Polymarket→Netflix jump.

Pricing Tiers + Task

Students see the three tiers and the task description before starting rounds.

Phase A: Individual Answer

Each student writes their own answer (min 20 chars) before seeing anyone else's.

Phase B: STOP — Group Discussion

The red STOP gate forces them to pause, talk to their group, and record what a groupmate said.

Phase C: Group Consensus

Students see their own answer and type a group consensus (min 30 chars). This repeats for all 5 Cs.

Ranking Screen

After all 5 rounds, students rank 1st–5th. Duplicates are flagged. This is where disagreement surfaces.

Summary Table (NEW)

NEW: Clean discussion table — all 5 Cs at a glance with Your Answer, Groupmate Said, Consensus, and Rank. Everyone pulls this up to compare.

Defend Your #1 (NEW)

NEW: Shows the student's #1 pick and asks them to defend it to their group. Great for sparking debate.

Key Takeaway

Final reflection: 'The most important C for Netflix's pricing is _____ because _____'

Section 2
Answer Key — The 5 Cs Applied to Netflix

For each C, the following includes the strong answer, what to listen for, and a common mistake to redirect.

C #1: Company Objectives
Question
Netflix created a $7.99 ad tier after years of being ad-free. What company objective does this reveal — are they prioritizing subscriber growth, revenue per user, or something else?
Strong Answer
Netflix shifted from pure subscriber growth to revenue diversification. They want both more subscribers AND more revenue per household. The $7.99 ad tier lowers the entry price to attract price-sensitive viewers, increasing total subscriber count. Meanwhile, Premium at $24.99 serves convenience-driven viewers willing to pay for ad-free. The ad tier also creates a second revenue stream from advertisers, reducing dependence on subscription fees alone. This is dual-axis optimization: more subscribers + more revenue per person.
Company Objectives
Listen For
Students who recognize the dual objective — it's not just "get more subscribers" OR "make more money per person," it's both simultaneously.
Common Mistake & Redirect
"They just want more subscribers."
Ask: "Then why did they also RAISE the price of Premium to $24.99 at the same time? That doesn't grow subscribers — it loses some. What else might they be optimizing for?"
C #2: Customers
Question
There's a $10/month gap between Standard with Ads ($7.99) and Standard ($17.99). What does this gap tell you about how Netflix views its two types of customers?
Strong Answer
Netflix sees two distinct customer segments with very different willingness to pay. Price-sensitive viewers will tolerate ads for a low price. Convenience-driven viewers will pay a premium for ad-free. The $10 gap is not based on cost difference (serving ads to a Standard tier doesn't cost Netflix $10 less per month) — it's based on perceived value and willingness to pay. The gap also creates an anchoring effect: $17.99 feels more reasonable when $7.99 exists as a reference point.
Customers / Willingness to Pay
Listen For
Recognition that the gap is strategic (value-based), not cost-based. Students who confuse "different features" with "different costs" need this clarified.
Common Mistake & Redirect
"It costs Netflix $10 less per month to serve ads."
Ask: "Does it though? What are the actual cost differences between serving an ad-supported stream and an ad-free stream? Think about: encoding cost, bandwidth, server load, support. Do those really change by $10 per user per month?"
C #3: Costs
Question
Netflix spends ~$17 billion/year on content. Every subscriber watches the same library. How do Netflix's costs change (or not change) as they add subscribers?
Strong Answer
Content is a massive fixed cost — it doesn't change per subscriber. Whether Netflix has 100M or 300M subscribers, they still spend ~$17B on content yearly. Variable costs (bandwidth, customer support, payment processing) are tiny per subscriber. This means every new subscriber is almost pure margin. This cost structure explains why growth matters so much — the more subscribers, the lower the per-subscriber cost, and the higher the profit per person. Netflix's entire business model depends on spreading fixed costs across as many subscribers as possible.
Costs / Fixed vs Variable
Listen For
Students who connect the cost structure to the growth imperative. They should recognize that fixed costs create leverage.
Common Mistake & Redirect
"Costs go up because more people are watching."
Ask: "Does Netflix create new content for each new subscriber? What changes when subscriber #300,000,001 signs up? What's the marginal cost of one more viewer?"
C #4: Competition
Question
Disney+ charges $9.99 (with ads) and $16.99 (no ads). Hulu starts at $9.99. Max starts at $10.99. Peacock starts at $7.99. How does Netflix justify charging $17.99 for Standard when competitors are cheaper?
Strong Answer
Netflix justifies premium pricing through perceived value, not price competition. They've positioned as the "must-have" service — the one people keep when they cancel others. Sources of differentiation: brand perception (Netflix = first-mover, most trusted), content library size and investment (Stranger Things, Wednesday, Squid Game), personalized recommendations (sophisticated algorithm), and switching costs (watch history, multiple profiles, cultural moments on Netflix). They also benefit from first-mover advantage, which built brand loyalty and scale advantages competitors can't easily replicate.
Competition
Listen For
Recognition that Netflix is not competing on price — they're competing on perceived value and switching costs. Students who say "Netflix has better shows" need to be pushed to articulate why.
Common Mistake & Redirect
"Netflix has better shows."
Ask: "Does it? Disney has Marvel and Star Wars. Max has HBO and Game of Thrones. Hulu has diverse originals. What specifically makes Netflix worth $7–10 more per month? Is it the shows themselves, or something else about the experience?"
C #5: Channel Members
Question
Netflix sells direct to consumers — no middlemen. But they now have an ad tier, which means advertisers are a new "channel partner." How does adding advertisers change Netflix's pricing calculus?
Strong Answer
With the ad tier, Netflix now has two revenue streams: subscription fees AND advertising revenue. Advertisers effectively subsidize the lower subscriber price — Netflix can charge $7.99 because advertisers fill the revenue gap. A $7.99 subscriber with ads might generate $12–15 in total revenue when ad dollars are included. The addition of advertisers introduces a new stakeholder whose interests (viewer data, attention metrics, maximizing ad impressions) may not always align with subscribers' interests (fewer ads, better experience). This creates new channel dynamics and potential conflicts.
Channel Members
Listen For
Students who see advertisers as a channel partner, not just a revenue source. The relationship creates new dynamics, new stakeholders, and potential conflicts.
Common Mistake & Redirect
"Netflix doesn't have channel members because it's direct-to-consumer."
Ask: "Who's paying for the ad tier to exist? Who negotiates with Netflix for ad placement? Are advertisers customers, partners, or channel members? What do they get in the relationship?"
Section 3
Concept Coverage Map

Explicit (Taught Through Exercise)

Concept Exercise Round How Tested
Company Objectives C #1 Students identify Netflix's dual objective (subscriber growth + revenue per user)
Customer Willingness to Pay C #2 Students analyze the $10 price gap as value-based, not cost-based
Fixed vs Variable Costs C #3 Students explain cost structure and why growth drives margin
Competitive Pricing C #4 Students justify premium pricing despite cheaper competitors
Channel Members C #5 Students analyze advertiser role in pricing calculus

Implicit (Surface During Debrief)

Concept Where It Surfaces
Value-Based Pricing The $10 gap is based on value, not cost (C #2)
Price Anchoring $7.99 tier makes $17.99 feel reasonable in comparison (C #2)
Revenue Diversification Ad tier creates dual revenue streams (C #1, C #5)
Leverage & Scale Fixed costs spread across more subscribers = higher margin (C #3)
Section 4
Professor Guidance for Discussion Questions

During the consensus phase of each C, use these prompts to deepen thinking and connect to course concepts.

C #1: Company Objectives — What to Listen For

In Group Discussion: Are they recognizing the dual objective? Do they see that Netflix is optimizing for two things simultaneously: more subscribers AND more revenue per user? This is the pivot from "growth at all costs" to "profitable growth."

Follow-up Question: "If Netflix ONLY cared about subscriber growth, why raise Premium to $24.99? What does that price tier tell you about what else they care about?"

Connection to Polymarket Case: Like Polymarket initially prioritized user growth over monetization, Netflix here is trying to do both. But the difference is Netflix is deliberate: they're using the ad tier to capture price-sensitive users AND raising Premium to capture high-value users. They're segmenting intentionally.

C #2: Customers — What to Listen For

In Group Discussion: Are they distinguishing between customer types? Do they see the $10 gap as strategic (targeting different willingness-to-pay) rather than cost-justified? Do they recognize anchoring (the low price makes the high price seem more reasonable)?

Follow-up Question: "If I showed you that Netflix's cost to serve a Standard with Ads subscriber is only $1 different from Standard ad-free, would that change your answer about the $10 gap? Why or why not?"

Connection to Polymarket Case: Polymarket initially treated all users the same (free platform for everyone). Netflix here is doing value-based segmentation: different prices for different customer preferences. This is the move from "one product, one price" to "multiple tiers, strategic segmentation."

C #3: Costs — What to Listen For

In Group Discussion: Do they understand the fixed-cost structure? Do they connect this to why Netflix is obsessed with growth? Do they see that content spend is sunk (it doesn't increase with more subscribers)?

Follow-up Question: "Netflix spends $17B on content whether they have 200M subscribers or 250M. But their revenue per subscriber is the same. So what happens to profit when they add 50M new subscribers?"

Connection to Polymarket Case: Polymarket's platform is also fixed-cost heavy (engineering, infrastructure, regulatory compliance). This is why both Polymarket and Netflix obsess over scale — once you've built the infrastructure, each new user is increasingly profitable.

C #4: Competition — What to Listen For

In Group Discussion: Are they looking beyond price alone? Do they recognize brand, switching costs, content investment, and recommendation algorithms as competitive moats? Do they see why Netflix can ignore Peacock's $7.99 price?

Follow-up Question: "Why can't Peacock just copy Netflix's strategy? They're owned by NBCUniversal (huge content), have the tech, and they charge $7.99. Why isn't everyone switching?"

Connection to Polymarket Case: Polymarket faces competition from traditional prediction platforms. But Polymarket's moat is different: transparency, blockchain-based settlement, community. Like Netflix, they're not competing on price (they're free!) — they're competing on trust, experience, and what's possible on their platform.

C #5: Channel Members — What to Listen For

In Group Discussion: Do they see advertisers as channel members with their own objectives? Do they recognize the potential conflict: advertisers want more data, more targeting, more impressions; subscribers want fewer ads, better experience? Do they see how this creates new pricing leverage for Netflix?

Follow-up Question: "Netflix now has two customers: subscribers and advertisers. If an advertiser wants to reach Netflix subscribers, who pays Netflix? And does that change how Netflix thinks about the subscriber experience?"

Connection to Polymarket Case: Polymarket also has channel members: market creators, traders, and potential future partners (exchanges, institutions). The difference with Netflix is that Netflix monetizes through ads (two-sided market); Polymarket monetizes through trading fees (one-sided). But both have to balance stakeholder interests.

Section 5
Common Student Mistakes & Redirects
  1. Analyzing each C in isolation
    What it looks like: Student says, "C #1 is about growth, C #2 is about segments, C #3 is about scale..." and doesn't connect them.
    Redirect: "Ask: How does what you said about Costs change your answer about Company Objectives? If Netflix has fixed costs, does that push them toward one objective or the other?"
  2. Confusing price with cost
    What it looks like: Student says, "The $10 gap exists because Netflix's costs are different for each tier."
    Redirect: "Is the $10 gap a cost difference or a value difference? What's the actual cost difference Netflix faces?"
  3. Assuming Netflix's only goal is subscribers
    What it looks like: Student says, "The ad tier is to get more subscribers."
    Redirect: "Then why did they RAISE Premium to $24.99 at the same time? Does that grow subscribers? What else might they be optimizing?"
  4. Saying Netflix has no channel members
    What it looks like: Student says, "Netflix is direct-to-consumer, so no middlemen."
    Redirect: "Who else gets a cut when Netflix runs ads? Are advertisers not a channel partner?"
  5. Treating competition as only price comparison
    What it looks like: Student says, "Peacock is cheaper, so Netflix should worry."
    Redirect: "If price were all that mattered, why isn't everyone on Peacock? What else is Netflix competing on?"
Section 6
If a Group Finishes Too Fast
Timing Note Groups typically finish the 5 Cs in 18–19 minutes. If a group finishes early (by minute 15), deploy one of these extensions to keep them engaged.
Extension 1: Ranking & Prioritization

"Rank the 5 Cs from most to least important for Netflix's pricing right now. Now — would your ranking change for a BRAND NEW streaming service with no content library and no subscribers? Why or why not?"

This forces them to think about which Cs are foundational vs. leverage-dependent.

Extension 2: Scenario Analysis

"Netflix just announced they're raising Standard to $19.99. Which C justifies this? Which C makes it risky? Would you make the same move? Why or why not?"

This applies the framework forward and makes it predictive, not just analytical.

Extension 3: Competitor Application

"Apply the 5 Cs to Disney+ OR Hulu. How would their Company Objectives, Customers, Costs, Competition, and Channel Members lead to DIFFERENT pricing than Netflix?"

This shows that pricing is not universal — it's context-dependent (each company's unique position in the 5 Cs).

Section 7
Facilitation Timeline
Pacing This timeline assumes you're running the exercise live with students entering the web interface together. Each C round takes about 3 minutes (Phase A: individual ~1 min, Phase B: STOP + discussion gate ~0.5 min, Phase C: consensus ~1.5 min). The ranking and summary follow all 5 rounds.
0:00–2:00
Setup & Password
Students enter password, then provide: Your Name, UC Email (@uc.edu), Group Number. Announce: "You're on your own device. You'll work through 5 rounds. Each round has three parts: you answer alone, we pause so you discuss with your group, then you write down what you agreed on together."
2:00–4:00
Instructions & Netflix Context
Exercise displays Netflix pricing tiers and company context. Students review before starting the 5 Cs.
4:00–7:00
C #1: Company Objectives
Phase A (Individual): Student answers individually (min 20 chars). Prompt: "What's Netflix optimizing for with their tiered strategy?"
Phase B (STOP): "Turn to your group. Share your answer. Write down what a groupmate said that was different from yours (min 20 chars)."
Phase C (Consensus): Group agrees and types shared answer (min 30 chars). Listen for dual-objective insight.
7:00–10:00
C #2: Customers
Phase A: Individual answer. Prompt: "Why would Netflix create a $10 pricing gap between tiers?"
Phase B: Share and record a different perspective.
Phase C: Group consensus. Key insight: gap is willingness-to-pay, not cost-based.
10:00–13:00
C #3: Costs
Phase A: Individual answer. Prompt: "What costs change if Netflix gains 50M more subscribers? What stays the same?"
Phase B: Share and record a different take.
Phase C: Group consensus on fixed-cost structure.
13:00–16:00
C #4: Competition
Phase A: Individual answer. Prompt: "Why can Netflix charge $17.99 when Peacock charges $7.99?"
Phase B: Share and record a different view.
Phase C: Group consensus on competitive moat.
16:00–19:00
C #5: Channel Members
Phase A: Individual answer. Prompt: "What does Netflix gain by adding advertisers as a channel partner?"
Phase B: Share and record a different interpretation.
Phase C: Group consensus (give this one extra time — students often struggle most with Channel). Key insight: advertisers unlock a second revenue stream and lower-tier subsidization.
19:00–22:00
Ranking the 5 Cs
After all 5 rounds complete, students rank the 5 Cs from most to least important for Netflix's pricing strategy using dropdowns (1st through 5th). Expect strong disagreement here — Company Objectives and Customers often compete for #1, with Channel Members ranked #4 or #5. This ranking is a natural segue into debrief.
22:00–25:00
Summary Table + Defend Your #1
Exercise displays a discussion table showing all 5 Cs at a glance (Your Answer, Groupmate Said, Consensus, Rank). Every student pulls this up on their screen to compare. Below the table: "Defend Your #1" — students see which C they ranked #1 and write why. This sparks immediate group debate. Finally, a "Key Takeaway" reflection (min 30 chars).
25:00–30:00
Class Debrief
Use the ranking data to surface disagreement. "Why did some groups rank Customers #1 and others Company Objectives?" is a high-leverage question. Reference the answer key debrief answers to connect Cs to each other.
Stay Flexible If a round sparks great discussion, pause for an extra minute. The ranking phase and debrief are flexible — if students are engaged, extend discussion. If groups finish the 5 Cs early, move to ranking immediately.
Section 7.5
The Ranking Step: Why It Matters

After completing all 5 Cs, students rank the five factors from most to least important for Netflix's pricing strategy. This is a crucial pivot point: while the individual and consensus answers are relatively uniform (most students arrive at correct interpretations), the rankings reveal genuine disagreement and reveal how students weight the Cs against each other.

Expected Ranking Patterns

  • #1 (Most Important): Typically Company Objectives or Customers. Strong groups argue for Company Objectives because it shapes all other decisions. Others argue Customers (willingness-to-pay) is the hardest constraint.
  • #2–3 (Middle Tier): Costs and Competition usually land here. Both are important, but students recognize they're enabled or constrained by Objectives and Customers.
  • #4–5 (Least Important): Channel Members often ranks lowest — students sometimes see it as a "nice-to-have" revenue stream rather than structural. This is a teaching opportunity.
Debrief Hook The ranking disagreement is gold for discussion. Ask: "Why did some groups put Customers first and others Company Objectives?" or "Who ranked Channel Members last? Why?" These questions force students to articulate their reasoning and surface the interdependencies between Cs.
Section 7.7
Participation Tracking & Webhook Integration
How It Works When a student completes the exercise (submits all 5 Cs, ranks them, and finishes), the web interface fires a webhook that includes: Student Name, UC Email, Group Number, and timestamp. This webhook is configured to send to an n8n automation (currently under development) which will eventually log participation in Canvas.

For now (manual tracking): UC emails are captured in the exercise. You can use these to verify who participated. Students must provide a valid @uc.edu or @mail.uc.edu email to complete the exercise.

Future state (automated): Once the Canvas integration is live, participation will be logged automatically. Check with your technical lead for the current status of the n8n → Canvas workflow.

Note: The webhook URL is configurable. If you need to change where participation data is sent, contact your administrator.

Section 8
Debrief Slide Answers (Week 11.1 Slides)

Use these answers to frame the debrief slide discussion and show students how the Cs reveal Netflix's pricing logic.

Debrief Q #1: "Which C matters MOST?"

Slide Answer: Customers + Competition

Why: Netflix's pricing power comes from understanding willingness to pay (Customers — the $10 gap, the tiers) and being able to sustain premium pricing despite competition (Competition — brand, content, switching costs). These two Cs together explain why Netflix can charge $17.99 when Peacock charges $7.99.

Debrief Q #2: "Which C did Netflix CHANGE with the ad tier?"

Slide Answer: Channel Members

Why: The ad tier added advertisers as a new stakeholder and revenue stream. This fundamentally changed Netflix's pricing structure: they can now subsidize a lower tier because ad revenue fills the gap. Before the ad tier, Netflix only had subscribers as a channel. Now they have subscribers AND advertisers.

Debrief Q #3: "What C protects the premium price?"

Slide Answer: Company Objectives (supported by Costs)

Why: Netflix's aggressive content investment ($17B/year) signals a commitment to quality and differentiation. That investment justifies the premium price — it's proof they're serious about being the best. The Costs structure (fixed, massive) also explains why they MUST maintain scale and margin to sustain that investment. Competitors without the same cost structure (or content budget) can't match Netflix's positioning, which protects the premium.

For Professor Only. Do not distribute to students.
This guide is confidential and contains answers, discussion prompts, and facilitation notes. Share exercise and debrief slides with students; keep this Professor Key private.
